title: Avoid frequently access non-existent files
description: ### What happens? Frequent access attempts to non-existent files will lead to Linux slab memory increased. This is a problem we discovered in our product. Here is a screenshot of the system monitoring. This line caused frequent attempts to access non-existent files. ### To Reproduce ``` create table t(id int, a text); insert into t select i, md5(i::text) from range(1, 20000000) tbl(i); set memory_limit to '256MB'; -- execute this sql forever select count(*) from t as t1, t as t2 where t1.id = t2.id; ``` ### OS: linux ### DuckDB Version: main ### DuckDB Client: linux ### Hardware: _No response_ ### Full Name: fanvanzh ### Affiliation: fanvanzh ### Did you include all relevant configuration (e.g., CPU architecture, Linux distribution) to reproduce the issue? - [ ] Yes, I have ### Did you include all code required to reproduce the issue? - [ ] Yes, I have ### Did you include all relevant data sets for reproducing the issue? No - Other reason (please specify in the issue body)
bug_features_seed: CREATE TABLE users(id INT PRIMARY KEY, username TEXT NOT NULL, email TEXT UNIQUE);
CREATE TABLE orders(order_id INT, user_id INT, amount DECIMAL(10, 2), FOREIGN KEY(user_id) REFERENCES users(id));
SELECT u.username, COUNT(o.order_id) FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id GROUP BY u.username HAVING COUNT(o.order_id) > 0;
key_bug_features: Frequent access attempts to non-existent files
